Most Sencha I tried previously is a lot stronger in flavor than this tea. I do, however, love that this comes from Japan instead of China.

Note that there are no English instructions on the package but I needed no instructions because I am familiar with Sencha. The ingredients are organic green tea from Japan.

In summary, this tea is just okay with hardly any flavor. I expected more at the current price of $24 for a 3.5 ounce package. I also expected instructions in English on the package.

title: Fresh crunchy chickpeas with a flavorful red pepper and chili flavor with a bit of heat
text:
These snacks are over packaged as they come in a box, within a box, within five other boxes and then five individually wrapped packages in each of the five boxes. In total, there are five boxes of five packages for a total of 25 servings.  Each serving is 120 calories with 110mg of salt, 5g of protein, etc. The ingredients are chickpeas, canola oil, tomato powder, salt, onion powder, red bell pepper powder, chili powder, and garlic powder.

My boyfriend and I tried these and think they are good. I personally find them too hot for my taste but my boyfriend did not think it was that bad. The current price of $23.99 comes to $.96 cents per serving. These are a product of Australia.

UPDATE - I gave these to my boyfriend's daughter, who is vegan, and she and her mom love them. I also had my Postman try these and he liked them. Therefore, these were a hit all around.

title: Fresh crunchy chickpeas with a flavorful sea salt and PREDOMINANT balsamic vinegar seasoning
text:
These snacks are over packaged as they come in a box, within a box, within five other boxes and then five individually wrapped packages in each of the five boxes. In total, there are five boxes of five packages for a total of 25 servings.  Each serving is 120 calories with 110mg of salt, 5g of protein, etc. The first several ingredients are chickpeas, canola oil, sugar, potato starch, white vinegar powder, etc.

My boyfriend and I tried these and think they are delicious. The predominant flavor is vinegar but it is not offensive. I personally hate vinegar and loved these. The current price of $23.99 comes to $.96 cents per serving. These are a product of Australia.

UPDATE - I gave these to my boyfriend's daughter, who is vegan, and she and her mom love them. I also had my Postman try these and he liked them. Therefore, these were a hit all around.

title: Fresh crunchy chickpeas with a flavorful lime and cracked pepper seasoning
text:
These snacks are over packaged as they come in a box, within a box, within five other boxes and then five individually wrapped packages in each of the five boxes. In total, there are five boxes of five packages for a total of 25 servings.  Each serving is 120 calories with 110mg of salt, 5g of protein, etc. The first several ingredients are chickpeas, canola oil, sugar, potato starch, white vinegar powder, etc.

My boyfriend and I tried these and think they are delicious. The current price of $23.99 comes to $.96 cents per serving. These are a product of Australia.

UPDATE - I gave these to my boyfriend's daughter, who is vegan, and she and her mom love them. I also had my Postman try these and he liked them. Therefore, these were a hit all around.

title: Pleasant tasting tea (actually a tisane) that brews to a clear golden pumpkin color
text:
Any drink that is made from herbal ingredients is a tisane and not a tea. Tea only comes from two main varieties of the camellia sinensis evergreen. Nevertheless, both my boyfriend and I feel this tisane is pleasant tasting. 

I brewed this with 176 degree Fahrenheit water and steeped one tea bag for four minutes. The only ingredient is dried pumpkin and it tastes like pumpkin or squash.

There are 35 tea bags in this package. The current price of $14.99 comes to $.43 cents per cup.

title: Nice matcha/vegetable flavor w/toasty overtones; brews light cloudy fluorescent green
text:
This tea almost looks like blades of grass that have been cut short with green matcha powder and popped rice. The ingredients are actually green tea, roasted rice (they spelled roasted wrong on the package), popped rice, and Matcha.

It has a strong vegetable aroma to me. I brewed one teaspoon at 185 degrees (bag says boiling water) for 2 minutes (bag says 40 seconds) in 10 ounces of water. The resulting brew was cloudy and was sort of fluorescent green and the flavor was light and vegetable with sweet overtones and a nice toasty finish.

I actually feel like I am drinking something very healthy when drinking this. I also love that this comes from Japan instead of China.

This can be brewed at least twice. I usually do not brew the same tea leaves more than once but a really good tea, I brew twice. Some people do more.

Note that the instructions on the package are somewhat well-written, which is also a plus. That is not always the case with teas that come from Japan or China.

In summary, I really like this tea. I do not agree with their suggestion to use boiling water as green teas are normally brewed with 185 degrees. At $14.99 per bag, it is not cheap and rightfully so considering the quality. I would have liked this to come in a resealable pouch, instead I have to use a clip to keep the package closed.

title: Strong vegetable fragrance when dry/light vegetable overtones as a tea; brews light cloudy yellow
text:
This tea almost looks like blades of grass that have been cut short. It has a strong vegetable aroma to me. I brewed one teaspoon at 185 degrees for 2 minutes (bag says 60 seconds) in 10 ounces of water. The resulting brew was cloudy, had some sediment, and was sort of fluorescent yellow and the flavor was light and vegetable with sweet overtones - almost like drinking spinach water.

I actually feel like I am drinking something very healthy when drinking this. I also love that this comes from Japan instead of China.

This can be brewed at least twice. I usually do not brew the same tea leaves more than once but a really good tea, I brew twice. Some people do more.

Note that the instructions on the package are well-written, which is also a plus. That is not always the case with teas that come from Japan or China.

In summary, I really like this tea. At $18.99 per bag, it is not cheap and rightfully so considering the quality. I would have liked this to come in a resealable pouch, instead I have to use a clip to keep the package closed.

title: Delicious, plump, fresh dates; 36 dates individually wrapped; lots of packaging
text:
The packaging on this is gorgeous and it is over packaged. There are 12 individual boxes with 3 individually wrapped dates in each box for a total of 36 dates per this entire order. The dates are plump, fresh, and delicious. Both my boyfriend and I tried them and loved them. Note that each date has a pit inside.

The current price of $14.99 comes to $.42 per date or $1.25 per gorgeous box of 3 dates each. This is a product of Jordan.

title: Pleasant tasting coffee that is easy to make; was not overly strong
text:
There are eight 0.42 ounce coffee tea bags in this box that makes eight cups of coffee. At the current price of $17.99, that comes to a very expensive $2.25 per cup for a home brewing.

I brewed one coffee tea bag in 10 ounces of boiling water, dunked it about four times, and let it steep for 3-1/2 minutes. The brew came out perfect. One can adjust their strength to taste. Both my boyfriend and I tasted it plain and with cream and we found this to be very good and pleasant tasting. It is not too strong or too weak. This is good for convenience or on the go but very expensive for daily use.

title: Lightly flavored saffron green tea that tastes like mint & brews to a slightly cloudy golden yellow
text:
There are 12 sachets in this tin. Upon opening the box I was hit with a whiff of what smelled like mint. The brew also tastes minty with a hint of what might be saffron. I brewed one sachet to about 8-10 ounces of 185 degree Fahrenheit water for 3 minutes. The brew came out a slightly cloudy golden yellow. Note that saffron is practically the last ingredient and it is the peppermint that comes through on this brew to me.

The current price of $19 comes to a costly $1.58 per cup. Note that this is a blend of sencha green tea from China and saffron from Afghanistan. The ingredients are organic sencha green tea, organic ginger root, organic lemongrass organic peppermint, saffron, and natural lemon flavor.

title: Jasmine aroma before brewing is intoxicating; tea is flavorful with a golden brew
text:
There are 28 (25 + 3 extra) tea bags in this box. Upon opening the box I was hit with an intoxicating aroma of Jasmine. I brewed one tea bag to about 8-10 ounces of 185 degree Fahrenheit water for 4 minutes. This tea has a nice jasmine flavor to it and is a clear golden amber brew. It is one of the most flavorful Jasmine teas I tried in a long time.

The current price of $9.81 comes to a reasonably priced $.38 cents per cup. Note that this is a blend of ceylon and other origin flavor green tea that is packed in Sri Lanka.

title: Subtly flavorful green tea with jasmine buds; brews to a golden amber
text:
There are 30 tea bags in this "dented-in-shipment" tin. I brewed one tea bag to about 8-10 ounces of 185 degree Fahrenheit water for 4 minutes. The brew came out a golden amber. This tea has a subtle jasmine flavor to it. It brews like green tea and looks like green tea. I like it.

The current price of $13.99 comes to a competitively priced $.47 cents per cup. Note that there are no brewing instructions on the tin. This tea comes from China and the website on the tin does not work.

title: Makes wonderful soup; I soaked them overnight & cook them for almost 2 hours
text:
These beans are great. They come in a little tub with a handle. I did have trouble popping off the plastic tab, however. There are 1lb 8oz of beans in this tub. They look like little white lima beans.

I soaked half of them overnight in cold water in my fridge. I then made soup the next day. I brought the beans (soup) to a boil and then simmered them on low for almost two hours before they softened to the right consistency. I checked them every half hour and they had an al dente starchy bite until the last 15 minutes. They were perfect when cooked for almost two hours.

These beans are delicious, clean, and fresh.

title: Green tea with a distinct smoky flavor; brews to a golden amber
text:
I brewed one teaspoon to about 8-10 ounces of 185 degree Fahrenheit water for 5 minutes. The brew came out a golden amber. This tea has a distinct smoky flavor to it. It brews like green tea and looks like green tea but is not one of my favorites. I am not partial to teas with smoky overtones and this one is definitely one of those.

The current price of $10.99 ($15.99 minus $5) comes to a very reasonable $2.75 per ounce since there are 4 ounces of tea in this bag.

title: Almost flavorless green tea; just a hint of smokiness; brews to a slightly cloudy golden yellow
text:
I brewed one teaspoon to about 8-10 ounces of 185 degree Fahrenheit water for 5 minutes. The brew came out a slightly cloudy golden yellow. This tea is almost tasteless but has a hint of smokiness to it. It brews like green tea and looks like green tea but is not one of my favorites.

The current price of $10.99 ($15.99 minus $5) comes to a very reasonable $2.75 per ounce since there are 4 ounces of tea in this bag.

title: Deep, bold flavor with a smoky finish; on part with Lipton black tea
text:
This is an oolong tea made from tea dust instead of full leaves like loose leaf oolongs. The tea bags smell a bit like dried fish to me which is off putting but thankfully the brew is much better.

There are 100 tea bags in the box and the triangular tea bags do not have a string. I brewed this with 195 degree water for five minutes. The tea expanded fully in the teabag. The brew is a deep amber color which is typical of teas that have ground up leaves. The resulting flavor profile is slightly smoky and it has a bold taste (and look) similar to black coffee or black tea.

In summary, this is not a favorite of mine. The quality is on par with typical Lipton tea bags but of the oolong variety. The current price of $26 ($40 minus 35%) comes to $.26 cents per cup which is very reasonable. If one is a Lipton tea bag fan, then this will probably be a hit.

title: Light golden brew that has smoky notes - 3.5 stars
text:
This is a typical oolong tea with smoky notes. This is a 3 ounce package and the tea is not tea dust, which is nice. It appears to be a partial or whole leaf; I cannot tell.

I brewed this with 200 degree water for 4 minutes. The tea expanded fully in the teabag. The brew is golden and clear. The resulting flavor profile is what I would call rustic--woodsy and smoky. I did not taste any nutty notes like the package says.

Overall, this is a nice oolong that supposedly is decaffeinated. Since I am not affected much by a cup of caffeine, I do not notice any difference. The current price of $16.95 comes to $5.65 per ounce.

title: Flavorful & fresh whole cumin seeds; tasted great in my meatloaf
text:
These whole cumin seeds are flavorful and fresh. I added it to my meatloaf and it tasted great and gave it a little more flavor. 

This is a one-pound container of seeds that has both a wide open mouth dispenser and a pencil-hole dispenser from which to dispense the seeds. The current price of $12.99 comes to $.81 cents per ounce.

title: Four times as large as the typical bay leaf & not very flavorful
text:
These leaves are super large. In fact they are four times as large as the McCormick Bay Leaves (see comparison photo). They have a nice aroma but there is hardly any flavor. I used this in a dish and I did not detect, at all, that typical bay leaf taste.

Also, what I found strange is that it lists the calories at 313 calories (see photo) for the entire box. It makes no sense as I thought bay leaves were calorie free nor are bay leaves supposed to be eaten; it's only a flavoring that is discarded after cooking. Also, who uses the entire box. However, with these, I may have to use the entire box to get the flavoring of one typical McCormick Bay Leaf. These are not for me.

title: Nice selection of 8 loose leaf teas w/tiny freebie; infuser included; wish packs were opaque
text:
This is a very nice selection of eight teas with one freebie serving of "reading nook blend balack tea." In addition, a heart infuser is included. It is not my favorite infuser but it works.

The tea I tried was delicious. I tried the strawberries and cream rose black tea. I brewed one heaping teaspoon for four minutes in boiling water. That tea brewed to a deep clear amber color. The predominant flavor was strawberry. All the teas have caffeine.

- Sweet Georgia Peach Black Tea with black tea, green rooibos, peach pieces, peach/maple/walnut essences, and calendula.

- Vanilla Latte Tea with organic black tea, honeybush team, ground cardamom, and vanilla essence.

- Comfort Blend Black Tea with black tea, cinnamon chips, orange peel, ginger, clove, and orange extract.

- Gratitude Blend Black Tea with black tea, orange peels, blue cornflowers, strawberries, raspberry leaves, bergamot oil, and strawberry essence.

- Huckleberry Happiness Black Tea with black tea, elderberries, aronia berries, black currants, blueberries, hibiscus, cornflowers, and huckleberry essence.

- Porch Sippin' Pecan Black Tea with black tea, cinnamon chips, pecan pieces, pecan essence, and safflower.

- Fog Cutter Black Tea with Assam black tea, Ceylon black tea, African black tea, Darjeeling black tea, and lemon peel.

- Strawberries & Cream Rose Black Tea with black tea, strawberry pieces, raspberry leaf, rose petals, and strawberry/vanilla essences.

The tiny one serving sampler is "Reading Nook Blend Black Tea" with black tea, rosebuds, lavender, chamomile, and natural flavor.

There are two negatives. First, the tea packages are clear. I would have liked them to come in a foil container that is not left visible to light as light degrades tea. Second, there are no ounces on the package. I assume these are half ounce packages or less.

The current price of $22.95 is competitively priced for this as it comes to $2.87per package. I believe each package will make about 5 cups of tea which would translate to $.57 cents per cup. This would make a good stocking stuffer.

title: Nutrition label is IMPOSSIBLE to read; like any other chia seeds I tried before
text:
Chia seeds are tasteless but add volume/filler to hot cereals and anything else one cooks with it like soups, etc. They are high in nutrition and are usually about 90 calories per tablespoon from what I can remember. Unfortunately, the nutrition label on this cannot be read. They should have a double label that can be opened and read. I took a photo of the label and expanded it and can make out that there is a lot of calcium, fiber, protein, and carbohydrates in this. Other than this I cannot read anything.

I put this in my cream of wheat and as can be seen from my photo, the black spots are the seeds. It expanded and made the cereal more dense but did not change the taste.

This is made in India. This performs like any other chia seeds I tried previously. However, I would never order this again simply because I cannot read the label.

title: Tasteless sludge; no cooking directions; no coconut; we threw it out after trying it
text:
This is supposedly a 13.9 ounce bag of chocolate coconut protein oats with the first ingredient of rolled oats then cocoa powder, brown rice protein, dates, coconut milk powder, chia seeds, coconut sugar, and sea salt.

The bag says this can be prepared hot, overnight, as muesli, or used in protein bars yet, at the time of this writing, there are absolutely no instructions on how to cook this either on the bag, Amazon site, or their website. Therefore, I decided to steam this like I normally steam oatmeal. I used 1/4 cup of this powder (it looks like a powder with only a minimal amount of oats) to 1/2 cup of water and steamed for 17 minutes. I could tell there were chia seeds in this because it did expand as is typical of chia. However, this looks like cocoa powder and, except for a faint hint of chocolate, this was utterly tasteless. Also, the texture is like eating sludge. Both my boyfriend and I tried a spoonful and barely swallowed it. We threw this out immediately.

Their first mistake is not to include basic instructions on how to make this hot or overnight on their package. Their second mistake is excluding actual coconut. There is no coconut in this package--only coconut milk powder and coconut sugar. The chia seeds are what hold this together and the oats are barely detectable. Another thing is that it was not easy to wash off my dishes. I washed my dishes by hand and I had to scrub this off; it reminded me of spackle.

We found this utterly disgusting.

title: Pretty but flavorless
text: These twist pops are large. There are 12 of them in the package.  Each twist is 90 calories each. Unfortunately, these are tasteless. I doubt a child would mind but, I personally like pops with flavor.

title: Pretty but flavorless; I rec’d 8 swirl pops
text: These lollipops are medium and not jumbo. Also, I received eight pops and not six. Each pop is 90 calories each. Unfortunately, these are tasteless. I doubt a child would mind but, I personally like pops with flavor.

title: Light golden brew that has floral notes
text:
This is a very nice oolong tea with floral notes. There are 15 tea bags in the box (which arrived smashed) in triangular tea bags. This is made of leaves and not tea dust like some other teas that come in teabag form.

I brewed this with 195 degree water for 3 minutes. The tea expanded fully in the teabag. The brew is lemon-colored and almost totally clear. The resulting flavor profile is floral. According to the box, the start is camellia, then cherry blossom, and finish mountain musts. I have no idea of those layers of flavor but I taste only a very nice floral brew that tastes good to me. My boyfriend likes it too.

Overall, I really like this tea. The current price of $9.89 comes to $.66 cents per cup.

title: Strong vegetable fragrance when dry/light vegetable overtones as a tea; brews light cloudy yellow
text:
This tea comes in 20 tea bags. It has a strong vegetable aroma to me. I know how to brew green tea but there are no good instructions on the package; it just says not to use boiling water. I brewed one tea bag at 185 degrees for 3 minutes in 10 ounces of water. The resulting brew was cloudy and sort of fluorescent yellow and the flavor was light and vegetable with sweet overtones - almost like drinking spinach water.

I actually feel like I am drinking something very healthy when drinking this. I also love that this comes from Japan instead of China.

I really like this tea. Usually good tea does not come in tea bags, however. The current price of $17.99 for 20 teabags comes to $.90 cents per cup.

title: Tea is very flavorful & mango really comes through but some people may have to avoid this tea
text:
This black tea is very flavorful to me. I prepared it hot with one heaping teaspoon to about 10 ounces of boiling water and let it steep for a little over 5 minutes. The brew is a beautiful deep amber. I can tell that this tea will be good iced. It reminds me of peach tea but in a mango version. I really like it.

The ingredients are black tea, ginger, mango pieces, calendula petals, sunflower petals, and natural flavors.

When I notice ingredients I am not familiar with, I do an Internet search on them. This package does not come with any warning. However, what I discovered is that calendula may be a problem for some. Note that those who are pregnant or wanting to become pregnant are advised not to drink tea with calendula. Also, calendula may THEORETICALLY interact with certain drugs such as BP meds. Since I take BP meds, I will not be drinking any more of this tea just to be on the safe side and that is a shame because it tastes so good.

title: Good ceremonial grade matcha; the powder is sweet and not bitter; but is it tested?
text:
My goto match is PANATEA ceremonial grade from Japan. This is also from Japan and I wanted to see how it compares.

It is important that Matcha be made with quality leaves from a trusted source in Japan that has their leaves regularly tested for lead and other contaminants. Selectively sourced Matcha not only tastes good but is supposed to be healthy too with lots of antioxidants and other components.

This matcha is very good. I used one heaping matcha scoop's (i.e. chashaku) worth with 3 ounces of 180 degree water and then whisk to a froth.This “electric green tea powder” makes a good, slightly frothy, vegetable, and slightly sweet brew. It also dissolves relatively well, although not as well as the PANATEA brand.

Unfortunately, I cannot find, either on the can or on the Amazon site, whether this matcha has been tested for contaminants. This is a very good ceremonial matcha and the current price of $17.90 ($19.90 minus $2) is a lot cheaper than PANATEA matcha which is double that for the exact same amount of 1.06 ounces.

For those who consume matcha regularly, I recommend getting the following items or the equivalent.
- [[ASIN:B07JDFK1LT chawan (match bowl)]]
- [[ASIN:B07J6PLBXJ chasen (matcha whisk) with chashaku (matcha scoop) and matcha spoon]]
- [[ASIN:B00OEKNMPC chasen naoshi (matcha whisk keeper / stand)]]

title: Strong vegetable fragrance when dry/light vegetable overtones as a tea; brews light cloudy yellow
text:
This tea almost looks like blades of grass that have been cut short. It has a strong vegetable aroma to me. I brewed one teaspoon at 185 degrees for 3 minutes (bag says 60 seconds) in 10 ounces of water. The resulting brew was cloudy and sort of fluorescent yellow and the flavor was light and vegetable with sweet overtones - almost like drinking spinach water.

I actually feel like I am drinking something very healthy when drinking this. I also love that this comes from Japan instead of China.

This can be brewed at least twice. I usually do not brew the same tea leaves more than once but a really good tea, I brew twice. Some people do more.

Note that the instructions on the package are in very poor English. I brew green tea the way I like it and do not follow their guidelines.

In summary, I really like this tea but at $25 it is not cheap and rightfully so considering the quality. I would have liked this to come in a resealable pouch. Also, it would have been great if the instructions were written in good English.

title: Caffeine-free “loose-leaf” tisanes with calories; not my cup of tea
text:
What a disappointment. There are four pouches of five varieties of "loose leaf tea" that require an infuser to be used. There is actually no tea in this tin. These are actually tisanes. Any drink that is made from herbal ingredients is a tisane and not a tea. Tea only comes from two main varieties of the camellia sinensis evergreen.

In total there are 20 "tea" pouches with loose ingredients. There are four each of apple crisp, snowy nights, winter wine, holiday cheer, and fall mornings. For example the apple crisp is made of apple pieces, marshmallows, apple slices, walnuts, and flavoring. It looks like a package I should be eating and not steeping. Also, there is no calorie count on the package but there are calories.

I brewed the apple crisp as instructed. I brewed it with boiling water and let it steep for 12 minutes. The "tea" was a dirty and cloudy pale yellow. It tasted horrible to me. I hated the brew so much I poured it down the drain. I won't be trying the others. Even though I am a tea purist, I have tried herbal tisanes that are wonderful. This is not  for me. The tin is the nicest part of this product.

title: A tisane with health benefits; brews to a golden caramel; absolutely delicious
text:
This is actually a tisane. Any drink that is made from herbal ingredients is a tisane and not a tea. Tea only comes from two main varieties of the camellia sinensis evergreen. Nevertheless, this tisane is excellent, healthy, smells and tastes fresh, and is absolutely delicious.   The packaging directions say to brew one to two tablespoons in a preheated teapot and steep it for 20-30 minutes. Cool it down for another 20-30 minutes and then drink it like tea (after straining). I used one tablespoon to about 12 ounces of water and followed the rest of the directions. It brewed to a beautiful golden caramel color just like a good cream soda. The flavor is outstanding to me and hard to describe--more like flowery with a hint of herbs. 

Based on my Internet search, willowherb leaf is full of Vitamin C and contains magnesium and B vitamins and supports the immune system and heart. Rhododendron adamsii is supposed to support the immune system as well and increase vitality among many other benefits. Rose Hip Fruits are rich in antioxidants, may reduce inflammation, etc. 

On the reverse side, willowherb leaf may cause nausea, indigestion, gas, diarrhea, constipation. Rhododendron adamsii may cause allergies, sleep problems, depression, and neurosis. Rose hip fruits may cause kidney stones, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, heartburn, stomach cramps,  fatigue and lithium toxicity. It is best to avoid herbal teas in general if breastfeeding or pregnant or if one has an intolerance to any ingredient in a blend. These recommendations are based on not having enough information to prove that herbal ingredients are safe. I personally did not experience any of this but I only had one cup so far and I almost wanted a second cup. It was so good. I can see where this tea can become addictive because it tastes so good but I personally believe this should be used as a treat and sparingly, especially until one knows if they have any reaction. All the ingredients are caffeine free.

The ingredients willowherb leaf, rhododendron adamsii, willowherb flowers, and rose hip fruits. It is a product of Siberia, Russia

I would have liked this in a resealable foil pouch to keep this free from light instead of a cellophane bag that rips immediately. I personally think this should be dispensed into another container to preserve the freshness. Other than this, this tea is delicious and healthy for many (probably in limited quantities) unless pregnant, breastfeeding, interfering with certain meds, or if one has an intolerance for the ingredients.

The current price of $11.79 for 1.76 ounces is competitively priced.

title: Has a strong vegetal fragrance when dry & light vegetal overtones as a tea; almost like a tea dust
text:
This tea almost looks like matcha or blades of grass that have been pulverized. It has a strong vegetal aroma to me. I brewed one teaspoon at 185 degrees for 1 minute (bag says 30 seconds to 1 minute) in 10 ounces of water. The resulting brew was cloudy and sort of fluorescent green and the flavor was light and vegetal--almost like drinking spinach water.

I actually feel like I am drinking something very healthy when drinking this. I also love that this comes from Japan instead of China.

Since this is grown under nets, the leaves are richer in chlorophyll which has more vitamins and antioxidants than other teas. It is also high in caffeine. It does not say on the package how many times the spent leaves can be brewed but I assume at least twice.

In summary, I really like this tea but at $35 it is not cheap and rightfully so considering how labor intensive it is to grow.

title: A tisane with health benefits; brews to a rosy amber hue; product of Siberia Russia
text:
This is actually a tisane. Any drink that is made from herbal ingredients is a tisane and not a tea. Tea only comes from two main varieties of the camellia sinensis evergreen. Nevertheless, this tisane is excellent and healthy too. I personally brewed this tea with one tablespoon to 12 ounces of boiling water and steeped for only 4 minutes then drank it. It was delicious. However, the packaging directions say to brew one to two tablespoons in a preheated teapot and steep it for 20-30 minutes. Cool it down for another 20-30 minutes and then drink it like tea. I was too impatient for that and I did not want my tea cold.

When measuring out the ingredients, note that the black currant leaf is not broken up, so it is important to include some of that with the "herbal sediment" on the bottom in order to make this brew. The brew is a rosy amber. It also tastes very good with mint being the predominant flavor.

Based on my Internet search, both sea buckthorn and black currant leaves are a source of vitamins and antioxidants and are believed to have blood pressure controlling properties as well as boosting immunity and skin benefits and promoting a healthy liver. On the reverse side, these ingredients (particularly sea buckthorn) "may interfere with certain medicines that treat gastrointestinal conditions, heart rhythm problems, cancer, or autoimmune diseases." It is best to avoid herbal teas in general if breastfeeding or pregnant or if one has an intolerance to any ingredient in a blend. Although there is no specific known harmful effect that I could find related to sea buckthorn or the other ingredients, the recommendation is based on not having enough information to prove that herbal ingredients are safe.

This is made from wild ingredients. The ingredients are black currant leaf, mint leaf, goldenrod, hibiscus, chamomile, sea buckthorn (leaf and berry), thyme, cornflower, and rose hip. It is a product of Siberia, Russia

I would have liked this in a resealable foil pouch to keep this free from light instead of a cellophane bag that rips immediately. I personally think this should be dispensed into another container to preserve the freshness. Other than this, this tea is delicious and healthy unless pregnant, breastfeeding, interfering with certain meds, or if one has an intolerance for the ingredients.

The current price of $11.79 for 1.76 ounces is competitively priced.

title: Smooth flavor profile that is barely sweet to me; just delicious; brews to a gorgeous caramel
text:
At first I did not find the directions on the package on how to brew this tea so I brewed it like any black tea with boiling water and steeped for 4 minutes. I used one teaspoon full in about 10 ounces of water. The resulting brew is a gorgeous caramel color and the flavor is smooth with just a hint of sweetness. I usually never brew tea more than once but I brewed this one two times and it was just as good as the first time. I absolutely love this tea.

Note that there are brewing directions on the Amazon site but they differ slightly from what is on the box; directions are not on the foli package. The Amazon site says to brew 0.18 ounces of tea in 10.1 to 11.8 fluid ounces of water at 194 degrees Fahrenheit and steep for 2 to 3 minutes and it can be brewed up to 3 times. The box says to use 5g tea with 350ml of 90 degree C water and soak to drink. Apparently 5g is equivalent to 0.176 ounces; 90 degrees C is 140 degrees F and soak to drink is a guess in minutes.

Note this comes with one serving of Cha Wu [A] Keemun Black Tea along with a clip to keep the tea fresh in its foil pouch.

I absolutely LOVE this tea. It has a lovely flavor, is very smooth and not harsh on the palate, and is a beautiful brew.

title: Subtle flavor profile that is slightly vegetal and barely sweet; brews to a faint clear yellow
text:
There are no directions on the package on how to brew this tea. However, I brew green tea with 185 degree water and steep for 4 minutes. Since the tea leaves are like grass strands, I used two teaspoons full in about 10 ounces of water. The resulting brew is just a whisper of golden yellow and the flavor is slightly vegetal with just a hint of sweetness. I usually never brew tea more than once but I brewed this one two times and it was just as good as the first time. I like this tea, although if it had a stronger flavor profile, I would have loved it.

Note that there are brewing directions on the Amazon site but if one is gifting this to someone else, they should be on the package. The Amazon site says to brew 0.18 ounces of tea in 10.1 to 11.8 fluid ounces of water at 190 degrees Fahrenheit and steep for 2 to 3 minutes and it can be brewed up to 3 times.

Note this comes with one serving of Osmanthus & Long-Jing tea along with a clip to keep the tea fresh in its foil pouch.

title: It is okay cold brew that is a little bitter; I prefer my other brand
text:
There are four cold brew pouches in this bag. Each pouch contains three ounces of pre-ground coffee. Per the instructions, I added one pouch to 24 ounces of water and steeped it in the fridge for 24 hours. That is not much water at all. However, once the brew was complete, I removed the pouch and added more water to taste.

The cold brew was okay but slightly bitter. It is not my favorite. I personally love the Green mountain cold brew that can be purchased [[ASIN:B07BMTX3QV here]]. That one is 5 stars.

title: Excellent black truffle sea salt; flavorful and better than I expected but not much truffle
text:
This black truffle sea salt is highly flavorful to me. I actually never liked black truffle when I tasted it several times previously due to its earthy flavor. However, this sea salt does not have that which means it is low on the black truffle. To me this salt is 5 stars but 3 stars for not living up to the earthiness of black truffle.

The ingredients are sea salt, black truffle salt (sea salt, dried truffle, natural and artificial flavors), spice (black pepper), and dehydrated mushrooms. This is a great combination and it is great to flavor rice. It would be great in soups too.

Personally, I love this salt but the flavor profile is more mushroom than black truffle.

title: This turned my batter purple but not the baked cupcakes – 2.5 stars for me
text:
I never heard of or tried purple potato flour before but I decided to try it for baking. I wanted to turn my white cupcakes purple, so I followed the directions and added one teaspoon of the powder. It turned my batter pink and it had an odd, sticky texture but I am not sure if it was the powder that did this or just the batter. Nevertheless, after baking, there was no purple to be seen and the cupcakes did not come out as good as usual.

This is a failure for me. After two other people tried the cupcakes, I had to throw them out and never had to do that before. I only got this to color my cupcakes purple. Perhaps it will work on bread but I don't bake bread. I would have given this one star but I believe this must work for something and since it is working for others, I rated this two stars.

title: Intense and fragrant pepper just as it should be; grinder required
text:
The flavor of this pepper is intense and highly fragrant. It smells and tastes fresh. It also grinds well. The only thing I do not like is the resealable pouch. That does not seal well and I prefer a plastic container instead.

Overall this is great black pepper. At the current price of $8.94 for .88 pounds (around 14 ounces), this comes to $.64 cents per ounce which is cheaper than I buy it for at my local store.

title: Utterly delicious with the right amount of heat; scrumptious on cream cheese
text:
This is utterly delicious. I was almost afraid to try this because I usually do not like anything hot. However, another reviewer said this was great on cream cheese so I tried it on that and it's wonderful. It would also be great on scrambled eggs as well. The jar says this pairs well with pork loin, grilled salmon, and charcuterie. However, I think eating this with cream cheese is the best because it tastes very similar to  Philadelphia Pimento Cream Cheese that used to be sold years ago.

The ingredients are red bell pepper, cane sugar, apple cider vinegar, habanero peppers, pectin, kosher salt, and citric acid. Note that habanero is spelled incorrectly as "habvanero" on the ingredients list. One tablespoon is a serving and that equals 60 calories with about 6 servings per container.

At the current price of $19.99 this is a very expensive $10 per 4-ounce jar but it is so tasty that I didn't deduct one star for the high cost. I consider this a craft item which always is more expensive than store bought items and usually better tasting too.

title: Highly fragrant pepper just as it should be; grinder required
text:
This is a highly fragrant pepper that smells and tastes fresh. It also grinds well. The container top has a punch out area so that this can be dispensed through the top, whole, and in various quantities. However, the top is cheap and I prefer to not punch this out so as to keep the pepper fresh. It is easy to use a funnel just to load this into my peppermill.

Overall this is great black pepper. At the current price of $11.99 for 18 ounces, this comes to $.67 cents per ounce which is cheaper than I buy it for at my local store.

title: 20 tea bags of decent green tea w/vegetal overtones; packet quantity & brewing directions wrong
text:
There are 20 tea bags in this foil packet but the wrapping on the packet says there are two times 20 tea bags, which is incorrect. Also the insert says to brew this green tea with boiling water. Anyone who regularly drinks various teas knows that brewing green tea in boiling water turns it very bitter and burns the leaves.

I brewed one tea bag in 185 degree Fahrenheit water for 4 minutes and it resulted in an orange-yellow brew that tasted slightly vegetal. To my palate this is decent tea considering it comes in a teabag.

The current price of $6.99 for 20 cups worth of tea (i.e. 20 tea bags) comes to a reasonable $.35 cents per cup of tea.

title: Fabulous spice combination; takes food up a notch
text:
This blend of salt, garlic, onion, black pepper, cumin, and soybean oil is incredible. Per the label, there are 12 one tablespoon servings per container at 20 calories each. For me, it will be a lot more servings than that.

However, this is great in soups and on fish, chicken, and vegetables. I tried it on my corn and it was excellent. This would be great with beans and rice and in a beef and barley soup. I just love it.

Of course one can make their own blend but why bother when it comes already prepared. This 5.9-ounce jar is competitively priced at $4.99.

title: Brews too light for a black tea but love the color; taste is barely detectable
text:
I never had Keemum loose leaf tea before. I brewed one teaspoon to 8 ounces of boiling water and steeped it for four minutes. The resulting brew was a clear golden amber--just beautiful. The flavor is barely detectable, however. Also, I expect a black tea to be darker. This looks more like an oolong tea.

The can says this is rich and smooth with a subtle smokiness and hints of cocoa. I taste none of that. It is not a tea I would order again.

title: Smells and tastes more like vanilla than hazelnut w/a strong finish but otherwise good
text:
There are six 11-ounce bags of vanilla hazelnut in the box with an expiration of April 3, 2022 which is about six months and two weeks from now. This is 100% arabica coffee with natural and artificial flavors.

The aroma and taste of this coffee is predominantly vanilla and less hazelnut. It is a decent blend but has a strong finish. I personally prefer hazelnut flavoring only after having tried this.

The current price of $49.99 comes to $8.33 per 11-ounce bag. There are 4.125 pounds in this six-bag set,  which comes to $12.12 per pound. Caribou coffee is a good brand but this one is not my favorite due to the flavoring.

title: Wonderful coffee from a pod; tastes like cold brew; not bitter; smooth & light taste
text:
Previously, I tried these [[ASIN:B092PJM1YD coffee tea bags]] and was disappointed. I wanted to try another brand, so I ordered these.

There are 30 individually wrapped coffee tea bags in the pouch good enough for 30 cups of coffee. I just removed the "coffee tea bag" from its package and spread its wings over my mug. I then ripped the top seam off so it looked like a drip coffee maker's open filter. Next I slowly poured boiling water over the pouch and let it steep for 5 minutes. The directions say for 3-5 minutes and to use 150 ml of water (5.072 fluid ounces) but I used about 8-10 ounces of water because I prefer a lighter flavored coffee.

It brewed to a beautiful and clear amber color. The taste was smooth and not at all bitter. In fact, it reminded me of a cup of cold brewed coffee but only it was hot.

I absolutely love this invention. There is a lot of packaging waste but if one wants a cup of joe on the go, then these portable pouches answer that call.

The current price of $12 ($24 minus 50% coupon) is a steal for this as it comes to .40 per cup. If it were the regular price it would not be such a bargain.

UPDATE - What I do notice is that sometimes the paper wings over the cup come undone and spring back to the coffee tea bag. I just press down on the rim where the wings are hanging over and that seems to help. This does not happen after the water is poured.

FINAL UPDATE: I had several people try this. One person was disabled with Parkinson's and found this too complicated to use. However, my Postman liked this because he said it was good for travel. He did not find the coffee to be as good tasting as I did but he overall liked this.

title: These are a bit on the mild side
text:
Mustard seeds are good for pickling, salad dressings, for potato salad, meats, etc. I use ground mustard seed in egg salad.

These seeds are lightly fragrant but they are a bit on the mild side. I chewed about one quarter teaspoon and barely detected any flavor punch. Therefore, these are just okay to me.

title: Almost tasteless tisane (for tea) but supposedly has health benefits; brewing temperature is wrong
text:
Any drink that is made from herbal ingredients is a tisane and not a tea. Tea only comes from two main varieties of the camellia sinensis evergreen. Nevertheless, this tisane is almost flavorless but it's apparently healthy. I brewed about 6 to 7 rosebuds (a heaping teaspoon's worth) in 185 degree water (not 70/80 degree water like the package says) and steeped it for five minutes. It brewed to a pale lemon hue and the flavor tasted so subtle that it was almost like drinking water.

I am giving this 3 stars because supposedly it has health benefits but I am disappointed that it lacks flavor. Recently, I tried these [[ASIN:B091BWBKNH OMIO]] rose buds, which were absolutely divine. In fact, I loved them so much, it made me want to try this tea.

Also note there are warnings on the package for those who are pregnant, menstruating, constipated, feel weak, or have diarrhea not to drink this tea.

In summary, the buds do have a fragrance but it is not the fragrance I expected. Also, they need to correct the brewing temperature on the package. When it says to pour 70 or 80 degree hot water, that is NOT hot. That is room temperature. I suspect they meant 170 or 180 degrees. I love the color of the brew but the taste is not there. The current price of $10.91 ($11.99 minus 9%) for 1.76 ounces is double the price and half the amount of the [[ASIN:B091BWBKNH OMIO]] rose buds I love.

title: Smooth flavored black tea matcha that can be worked into a good froth but no certification
text:
My go to green tea matcha is Panatea ceremonial grade matcha but  I never heard of black tea matcha before. However, all matchas are made from the ground up tea leaves of the camellia sinensis evergreen. As opposed to regular tea where the leaves are steeped, the leaves of matcha are actually being consumed in the form of a dissolved powder of tea leaves. This is why it is so important to have these leaves tested to ensure there are no arsenic, metals, and other contaminants.

Unfortunately, there is no information on the box or the Amazon site as to whether this has been tested or not. I am going to assume it has been but I've taken off one star due to the lack of information.

In the box are three black tea matchas--my first experience into this realm.

- Chai Matcha - Source is India / Kenya and ingredients are black tea, ginger, cinnamon, cardamon, black and white pepper, clove, and nutmeg.

- Black Organic Earl Grey Matcha - Origin is Sri Lanka and ingredients are organic black tea and natural flavors

- Organic English Breakfast Matcha - Origin is Sri Lanka and ingredients are black tea and natural flavors

I tried the Earl Grey one and it was very good. The flavor was smooth, not bitter and not gritty. The flavor of the earl grey was subtle but it was there. Instead of using one full teaspoon to a cup of boiling water, I made one quarter that amount and whisked it up using a matcha whisk. Since I was able to get a lot of froth, that tells me this is truly fine ground, which is great. This explains why there is some powder seepage outside the can during shipment. Note that this also dissolved well as long as I mixed it well. I have no idea if this would be ceremonial or culinary grade black tea matcha or even if there is such a distinction in the black tea realm as there is with green tea matcha. However, it behaves like ceremonial grade green tea matcha.

In summary, I really like this matcha. It is important that this be made with quality leaves that are certified free of harmful ingredients as matcha is made from ground tea leaves that are consumed. I do hope they update their site or include a comment on their packaging that this has been tested.  Matcha, such as this, not only tastes good but is supposed to be healthy too with lots of antioxidants and other components. The current price of $15.95 is competitively priced for three one-ounce containers as it comes to $5.32 per can. 

For those who consume matcha regularly, I recommend getting the following items or the equivalent.
- [[ASIN:B07JDFK1LT chawan (match bowl)]]
- [[ASIN:B07J6PLBXJ chasen (matcha whisk) with chashaku (matcha scoop) and matcha spoon]]
- [[ASIN:B00OEKNMPC chasen naoshi (matcha whisk keeper / stand)]]

title: The flavor is not particularly expressive; tastes somewhat artificial with burnt overtones
text:
There are 24 decaf K-cups in this box and the flavor is supposedly coconut rum. I barely taste the coconut and what I do taste has an artificial flavor to me. I definitely do not taste any rum. Instead this cup of decaf tastes like artificial coconut with a burnt finish. It is a little better with cream. I'll pass on this.

The current price of $14.79 comes to $.62 cents per cup.

title: Tea is almost tasteless but I love the golden yellow color of the brew
text:
Calendula tea supposedly has health benefits. Based on my Internet research, Marigold and Calendula are not one and the same. Therefore, I assume this Marigold tea is actually pot marigold which is calendula because it says calendula in the description and brewing instructions on the Amazon site.

I brewed two teaspoons of dried flowers in one cup of boiling water and let it steep for 20 minutes with the top of my tea mug covered to keep in the warmth. The resulting brew was a beautifully clean golden yellow. Unfortunately, it is almost tasteless as a tea but I do detect a chamomile-like aroma. It is as if I am drinking water, which I happen to love. Next time I will brew three teaspoons (i.e. one tablespoon). There are no instructions on the bag.

Per the bag, this can be used for aromatherapy, in potpourri, bath salt blends, etc.

In summary, the petals smell great and the brew is beautiful but the tea is practically flavorless. Nevertheless, there are a lot of health benefits in drinking this tea.

title: Fresh and smooth w/a hint of grassy flavor; brew is cloudy & light lime yellow
text:
I brewed one teaspoon of this tea at 180 degrees for 1 minute per the instructions. Personally, I prefer to brew tea for 3-4 minutes. The brew is cloudy and a faint lime yellow color. The flair is as subtle as the brew with a grassy/vegetal overtone. 

This is pretty good tea and I love that it is from Japan. The current price of $65 is expensive but typical for 3.5 ounces of a good tea.

title: I use in place of vermicelli for texture in my soup
text:
Sometimes I want to use vermicelli in soups instead of noodles. I decided to use these instead. They are chewy like they are supposed to be. I boiled them for six minutes and they were ready. They will be tasteless unless someone knows how to prepare them. Since I do not like Asian spices, I use them for texture and not taste and they are perfect. I love the chewy bite.

There are 13 servings per package and the package is resealable which is a plus. I would definitely order these again although I suspect I can get them cheaper than $9.99 per package at my local Asian store.

title: Fresh and vegetal; tastes like spinach water; brew is cloudy lime yellow
text:
I brewed one teaspoon of this tea to 8-10 ounces of water that was heated to 185 degrees. Note that there are no instructions on the package but this is the typical brewing temperature for green tea. The resulting brew was a cloudy lime yellow and the flavor is highly vegetal. It reminds me of the taste of spinach water. I definitely like this tea as it is fresh tasting with sweet overtones and since it is green tea, very healthy.

The current price of $6.75 ($9 minus 25%) for this 1-3/4 ounce package is reasonably priced. This is grown in Japan.

title: Highly fragrant; great for recipes, ham, in a potpourri mix, or as a tea
text:
These cloves are excellent and highly fragrant. They are great in recipes, ground, for use when baking ham, in potpourri, or as a tea. Note these are made in India and are organic.

I used this to make "tea." I ground a teaspoonful in a mortar and pestle and then boiled it in a cup of water for 10 minutes. I then strained the sediment and drank the liquid. It was very flavorful.

title: NOT HERBAL; tea comes w/everything except for the boiling water; price is reasonable for convenience
text:
I thought I was just ordering packages of tea but this is innovative and new and not only tea. There are four long boxes in the package. Three of the boxes contain cups with a mesh bottom that holds the loose tea leaves in place. The last box contains the lids for the cups. There is a lot of packaging waste but using this is no different than going out for a cup of tea.

There are three flavors with 10 cups each flavor of lychee black tea, peach oolong tea, and peppermint green tea. Note that this is not herbal as tea has caffeine and herbal tisanes do not. The addition of the flavoring is herbal such as green tea combined with dried peppermint leaves.

INGREDIENTS
- peppermint green tea - green tea and dried peppermint leaves
- peach oolong tea - oolong tea leaves, dried peach, dried chrysanthemum petals, and natural flavoring
- lychee black tea - black tea leaves, fine dried fruit pieces, and natural flavoring

To prepare the tea all I had to do was boil water, put a tiny bit in the cup to cover the mesh, swish the water gently around, then top off the entire cup with boiling water and cap it with the lid.

This tea tastes good enough to drink plain, like two steps up from Lipton tea. Also, the current price of $19.99 comes to a reasonable $.67 cents per cup considering everything except for the boiling water is supplied.

The only negatives are:
- There is a chance the tea will become bitter because the leaves cannot be removed.
- The tea container is very hot to the touch if boiling water is used.
- The flip opening on the lid does not clip into the cup lid. If the opening tab is played with too much, it actually folds into the lid, which means I have to remove the lid to push it back out again.
- There's a lot of packaging waste with this.
- The packaging has misspellings on it. For example "waring" for "warning" and "containg" for "containing."

Other than this, I really love this invention and am amazed at how the mesh stays put in the bottom of the cup.

title: Deep, bold flavor with a smoky finish; on part with Lipton black tea
text:
This is an oolong tea made from tea dust instead of full leaves like loose leaf oolongs. The tea bags smell a bit like dried fish to me which is off putting but thankfully the brew is much better.

There are 100 tea bags in the box and the triangular tea bags do not have a string. I brewed this with 195 degree water for five minutes. The tea expanded fully in the teabag. The brew is a deep amber color which is typical of teas that have ground up leaves. The resulting flavor profile is slightly smoky and it has a bold taste (and look) similar to black coffee or black tea.

In summary, this is not a favorite of mine. The quality is on par with typical Lipton tea bags but of the oolong variety. The current price of $32 comes to $.32 cents per cup which is reasonable. If one is a Lipton tea bag fan, then this will probably be a hit.

title: Flavorful peach oolong; brews to a lemon-colored hue
text:
This is a very nice oolong tea with a hint of peach. There are 20 tea bags in the box in corn fiber triangular tea bags. This is made of leaves and not tea dust like some other teas that come in teabag form.

I brewed this with 195 degree water for five minutes. The tea expanded fully in the teabag. The brew is lemon-colored and almost totally clear. The resulting flavor profile is slightly vegetal with a hint of peach. The oolong tea really comes out in this brew. Overall, I do like this tea very much.

The current price of $16.99 ($19.99 minus 15%) comes to $.85 cents per cup.

title: I love Panatea's Matcha so much I licked the “dust” off the scoop; sourced from China – 4.5 stars
text:
I ordered Panatea's matcha in the past and absolutely loved it because it has a sweet and not bitter flavor profile. In fact their ceremonial grade matcha is my favorite matcha. Around the 2013 timeframe I tried matcha for the first time and was so put off by the bitter brew that I did not pursue this type of tea again until 2016 when I first purchased Panatea's matcha.

This can is a full pound of ground culinary grade matcha and it tastes almost as good as the first day I tried their brand back in 2016. I used one scoop (supplied) which is the equivalent of 1/3rd teaspoon of matcha powder to about 12 ounces of boiling water. The instructions say not to exceed one teaspoon per cup. I then whisked up the brew into a slight froth. Note that this does not froth as well as ceremonial grade matcha, so the froth was more flat.

It is important that the matcha is made with quality leaves that are certified free of harmful ingredients as matcha is made from ground tea leaves that are consumed. When I contacted PANATEA back in 2016, they assured me they use Tencha leaves that are granite ground from a trusted source in Japan for their ceremonial matcha--note this matcha is culinary grade, so it's different. They also assured me at the time that they have the leaves regularly tested for lead and other contaminants. Per the Amazon site, they are still testing their tea; however, I question the source of this matcha. In one of the Amazon images, it says this is from the Fujian hills of China and in another image it says there is no Fukushima radiation--Fukushima is in Japan, not China. This makes me question the source of this tea because if it comes from China there is no reason to test for Fukushima radiation. There is no information inside the box or on the container other than to say it is made in China.

Nevertheless, selectively sourced Matcha, such as this, not only tastes good but is supposed to be healthy too with lots of antioxidants and other components. It also has a sweet taste that is a bit bolder than their ceremonial grade matcha but is still not bitter. 

This matcha is very good and I did lick the scoop clean. I refrigerated it after opening the can to keep the tea fresh.

The current price of $27.50 is competitively priced for this one pound container of culinary grade matcha. I do wish, however, that this was made with tea leaves from Japan rather than from China. When I purchased their ceremonial grade Japanese matcha in a 1.06 ounce can back in 2016, I paid $34. Of course, ceremonial grade is a higher grade than culinary grade so that is the reason for the higher price. Ceremonial grade is designed to be consumed on its own but culinary grade is usually used as an addition to something else like lattes, baked goods, shakes, etc. It can also be consumed like ceremonial grade but that is not how it is typically consumed. I will be consuming this like ceremonial grade matcha, however.

For those who consume matcha regularly, I recommend getting the following items or the equivalent.
- [[ASIN:B07JDFK1LT chawan (match bowl)]]
- [[ASIN:B07J6PLBXJ chasen (matcha whisk) with chashaku (matcha scoop) and matcha spoon]]
- [[ASIN:B00OEKNMPC chasen naoshi (matcha whisk keeper / stand)]]

title: Fragrant and fresh; perfect for soups, as salad topper, in baked bread, etc.
text: These seeds are fragrant and fresh and very small. They are perfect for soups, as a salad topper, or baked in bread. The current price of $7.99 for 7 ounces is very reasonable for this as it comes to $1.14 per ounce. Overall, these are very good.

title: At least 60 to 70% crumbs; cookies are nothing special - we'll pass
text:
My boyfriend and I tried these. He said these are nothing special and did not care for them. I think they are okay. Even though they are packaged in a box that is packaged in another box, and even though I gently cut open the package down the side, the cookies were still mostly crumbs with only about three fully in-tact ones in the package. After trying these, neither one of us would buy them.

There are supposedly four servings in this package with two cookies each serving. This means there are eight cookies in the package. To have only three of eight cookies arrive whole in one package is very disappointing. Each serving is 100 calories with 9g fat, 10mg cholesterol, 70mg sodium, 9g carbs, and 2g protein. There are four packages in the box which means there are supposed to be 32 servings. However, one never gets that amount because each package is mostly crumbs.

Ingredients: almond flour, erythritol, butter(cream, flavor), sugar free chocolate (unsweetened chocolate, cocoa butter, stevil glycosides (stevia extract) sweetener,sunflower lecithin (an emulsifier)), cocoa powder processed with alkali, palm oil, natural flavoring, eggs, egg whites, sea salt,guar gum, xanthan gum, stevia, and monkfruit extract. It contains eggs, milk, and almonds.

UPDATE - We disliked this so much that we threw out the other three packages.

title: Has an intoxicating vegetal fragrance when dry and light vegetal overtones as a tea
text:
This tea looks like blades of grass. It has an intoxicating vegetal aroma to me. I brewed two teaspoons at 185 degrees for 4 minutes in 10 ounces of water. Since the tea does not really fit on a teaspoon, that is why I used two teaspoons because the "blades" lay on top when scooping it out of the bag. The resulting tea had a pale yellow hue to it and the flavor was light and vegetal.

In summary, I really like this tea and $9.99 for 4 ounces is cheap for this.

title: Excellent tisane (for tea); tastes almost as fragrant as it smells but needs a package label
text:
Any drink that is made from herbal ingredients is a tisane and not a tea. Tea only comes from two main varieties of the camellia sinensis evergreen. Nevertheless, this tisane is excellent and healthy too. I brewed about 6 to 7 rosebuds (a heaping teaspoon's worth) in boiling water and steeped for four minutes. It brewed to a pale lemon hue and the flavor tasted subtly like the rose itself. I absolutely love this.

Based on the Amazon site, this can be used to make rose soap, as a decoration for baking, to add fragrance to bath water, etc. I periodically frequent a tea place that puts these buds on their clotted cream.

There is only one negative about this. I want the package to have a label with the ounces and the instructions for use; such as how to brew a tea, links to soap making, etc. Particularly how to brew tea. There is no identifying information on the packet other than the AMAZON SKU code.

The current price of $11.99 for 3.5 ounces is competitively priced.

title: Just like steeping tea but coffee is very weak
text:
There are 10 coffee tea bags in the box good enough for 10 cups of coffee. I just put 8 ounces of boiling water into a cup and let it steep for 5 minutes. The directions say for 3-5 minutes. The brew looks foamy while it is steeping. After I was done steeping, the resulting brew was light with a slight ring of sediment around my cup. I tried it both without half and half and with and was not impressed as it is too weak. My boyfriend also tried this and also said it was weak.

The current price of $14.99 for 10 is expensive for this as it comes to $1.50 per cup.

title: Absolutely delicious but chewy and there is always a wad that I have to spit out – 3.5 stars
text:
These air-dried beef slices are absolutely delicious to me. It reminds me a bit of dried pastrami. They are also very chewy, which I actually like. However, no matter how much I chew there is always a wad of connective tissue that I had to spit out because it is impossible to chew through it. Nevertheless, this is so tasty I ate an entire package in one sitting.

There are 90 calories per serving or 175 per package. What is interesting is that a serving is one ounce and the package is two ounces, yet eating the package is 5 calories less than eating two servings (which is the package) at 90 calories each. There are 430 mg of sodium per serving or 870mg (why not 860mg) per package and 16 g of protein per serving and 32 per serving.  The nutrition label numbers should be double the number of the individual servings but they are not.

The ingredients are Beef,Vinegar, contains 2% or less of: Salt, RaisinJuice Concentrate, Black Pepper, WhitePepper, Garlic Powder, Coriander, Clove, and Nutmeg.

There are three two-ounce bags in this package and at the current price of $14.35 comes to $4.78 per package which is expensive.

UPDATE: My boyfriend ate one of the three packages and he absolutely loves this. He said, "Wow, this is delicious."

title: Brews to a pale yellow color & has a clean, smooth, and fragrant flavor profile
text:
This tea is very good. It has a clean, smooth, and fragrant but very subtle flavor and a pale yellow brew. The flavor reminds me of a higher end tea similar to what is served in a Chinese restaurant. The leaves are rolled just like jasmine green tea pearls and one teaspoon expands to about four teaspoons of leaf after the tea steeps.

This came in a tin with an interior lid and in a shrink-wrapped foil. There are no instructions on the package as to how to brew this. Oolong tea is usually brewed between 190 and boiling depending on what the package says. I decided to steep one teaspoon in 195 degree water for 4 minutes and the results were good. I might brew this at 185 next time as this looks more like a green tea than an oolong.

Overall, this is a good tea and fresh too and the flavor is good. The current price of $22 ($24 minus $2) is pricey for 2.6 ounces of tea yet it is typical of the price many good teas command..

title: Good tisane; predominant flavor is chamomile and orange; I don’t taste mint - 3.5 stars
text:
This blend combines a real tea with a tisane. Any drink that is made from herbal ingredients is a tisane and not a tea. Tea only comes from two main varieties of the camellia sinensis evergreen. However, this actually combines tea (the Darjeeling green tea) with herbal ingredients. The ingredients in order are Darjeeling green tea, mint, chamomile, orange peel, and lemon verbena. This tea is from India.

I brewed this with boiling water and steeped one teaspoon for four minutes. Note there are no steeping times on the package. The resulting brew has a slightly cloudy lemon yellow hue and is fresh and fragrant with a balanced and light flavor that tastes predominantly of chamomile with slight orange overtones. I do not taste the mint; however, I prefer this tea's flavor over mint any day.

They need to mark the weight on the package as there is nothing on the tea envelopes that says how many ounces of tea are in each one. However, the Amazon site says each package weighs 3.53 ounces and there are two packages in this set. The current price of $17.98 ($19.98 minus 10%) comes to a reasonable $8.99 per package.

title: Tea has a balanced smoky flavor; it is an acquired taste
text:
Back in the 1990's I had a very close friend who loved Lapsang Souchong tea and introduced me to the brew. I could never understand why my friend liked it because it is a very smoky tea. Nevertheless, I ordered this in his memory because I want to acquire a taste for it and, believe me, this tea is an acquired taste.

This Lapsang Souchong is five stars to me; however, I remember the tea I tried with him being smokier and that is the only reason I took off one star. I brewed one teaspoon of this tea in eight ounces of boiling water for three minutes. The resulting liquor is a beautiful amber brew. The taste is smoky yet balanced.

The ingredients are lasang souchong leaves which I assume means black tea that has been smoked over red pine.

Note that this product is manufactured in a facility that also processes milk, eggs, fish, wheat, tree nut ingredients, and soybeans.

This tea is excellent. Per the bag, it is third party tested, GMP (good manufacturing practice) certified, non-GMO, and vegan. It is manufactured for a California company; however, it does not say where the tea is sourced from. Currently, Lapsang Souchong tea from China is outlawed in Europe because it contains residues from the smoking process that is not compliant with the EU standard. I have no idea if this tea falls into that category since there is no country of origin identified on the bag. I did contact them via the email address on the bag and am awaiting a reply.

The current price of $10.95 for 8 ounces of this tea is reasonably priced.

UPDATE: The manufacturer got back to me (see below) and this tea is sourced from China and manufactured in the US; therefore, it probably falls into the Eurpean prohibited category.

"Hello Kathleen,

Thank you for reaching out and bringing this fact to our attention. Currently, our Lapsang Souchong is sourced from China and manufactured here in the U.S. Please let us know if you have any other questions.

Thank You
AH
Nature Restore"

title: Coffee is smooth and medium light; it has a good taste
text:
This whole bean coffee smells great. I ground it into a medium grind and brewed 2-1/2 coffee scoops (i.e. 5 tablespoons) to 7 cups of coffee. It came out great. My boyfriend had this with half and half and it tasted great. 

This coffee is smooth and has a light flavor. We both love it. This is great coffee for those who do not like bold blends. 

The current price of $12.49 ($14.49 minus $2) is about right for 12 ounces of coffee.

title: Slightly thick and creamy but tastes very starchy
text:
There are two 10 ounce jars in this package. This is creamy and slightly thick but also runny. My boyfriend and I tried this on a small cuke and it was okay; we neither like it or hate it. However, it is not something I would order again. It has a starchy taste/texture to this.

This is 160 calories for two tablespoons with 5g of protein, 1.5g of saturated fat, 5g of sugar, 30g of sodium, etc. The ingredients are dried roasted milled chickpeas, roasted sunflower seeds, organic light brown sugar, olive oil, organic palm oil, and sea salt.

The current price of $14.99 comes to a slightly pricey $7.50 per jar.

title: Good tisane; tastes like a chamomile & lemon verbena blend should
text:
Any drink that is made from herbal ingredients is a tisane and not a tea. Tea only comes from two main varieties of the camellia sinensis evergreen. Nevertheless, this tisane is good. The Olympus Mountain Tea Sideritis scardica, lemon verbena, and chamomile dried flower buds are fresh and fragrant and brew to a pale lemon hue. The flavor is balanced and light. 

I brewed this with boiling water and steeped two teaspoons for five minutes. The ingredients are Organic Olympus Mountain Tea Sideritis scardica, organic chamomile and organic lemon verbena. This tea is from Greece. This is the first time I had Sideritis scardica as part of a herbal blend. Apparently, this is used as a herbal tea for inflammation and gastric disorders. 

The current price of $14.96 for 1.76 ounces is on the high side of competitively priced.

title: Brews to a whiskey amber color & has a clean & smooth flavor profile; love the leaf
text:
This tea is very good. It has a clean and smooth but very subtle flavor and a clear whiskey amber color. It is organic oolong tea and is full leaf, which I love.

Note that there are no instructions on the package as to how to brew this. Oolong tea is usually brewed between 190 and boiling depending on what the package says. I decided to steep this in close to boiling water. I brewed this at 208 degrees Fahrenheit for 4 minutes and the results were good.

This came with a tiny teaspoon. I actually used four of these "scoops" to make one cup of tea and that was equivalent to two teaspoons.

Overall, this is a good tea and fresh too. However, the flavor is extremely subtle. Mine was produced June 6, 2021. The current price of $9.99 is very reasonable for 3.5 ounces of tea.

title: Good tisane; tastes like chamomile should; may contain nuts
text:
Any drink that is made from herbal ingredients is a tisane and not a tea. Tea only comes from two main varieties of the camellia sinensis evergreen. Nevertheless, this tisane is excellent. The chamomile dried flower buds are fresh and fragrant and brew to a pale lemon hue. The flavor is balanced and light. There is some sediment at the bottom of the pouch, but that is to be expected. I do wish the pouch was not clear so that this is not exposed to light.

I brewed the chamomile with boiling water and steeped two teaspoons for 6 minutes. There are no instructions on the package on how to brew this. The only ingredient is chamomile but the package says this may contain traces of tree nuts, wheat, peanuts, and soybeans.

The current price of $5.99 for 1 ounce is on the high side of competitively priced and is about $2 more per ounce than this excellent [[ASIN:B08WKM784F chamomile tisane]]. If the price were cheaper, instructions were printed on the package, and the pouch was not clear on one side, this would have received 5 stars from me.

title: Brews to a pale yellow and has a vegetal flavor profile; love the leaf
text:
I usually like my green teas flavored such as Jasmine green tea. However, this tea is very good. It is organic green tea and is full leaf, which I love.

Note that there are no instructions on the package as to how to brew this. Green tea should never be steeped in boiling water. I brewed this at 185 degrees Fahrenheit for 5 minutes. The brew is a pale yellow and the flavor is vegetal and herbaceous as it should be.

This came with a tiny teaspoon. I actually used three of these "scoops" to make one cup of tea and that was equivalent to a teaspoon.

Overall, this is good green tea and fresh too. Mine was produced June 6, 2021.

title: Excellent; just enough heat to flavor eggs, chicken, rice, etc.
text:
This is excellent salt. There is just enough heat to flavor eggs, chicken, rice, soups, and other dishes. I used this on my chicken and it was excellent.

This is a 3.5 ounce grinder jar. I had to remove the inner seal, reseat the cap, open the top, turn over, and grind the amount desired onto my food. Note that the cap is sort of two separate pieces in the sense that the lower flat end is what gets secured onto the rim and tightened tight. The top round end turns forever. Therefore, when seating the cap onto the jar, make sure to hold only the lower collar on the cap to tighten. I did not do that originally and I was turning forever.

The ingredients are 91% sea salt and 9% African habanero chili. The current price of $9.95 is competitively priced for an online sale.

title: More like everything bagel seasoning; not very spicy; needs a dispenser
text:
There are two 2.5 ounce jars of "spicy everything salt" in this package. Note that salt is the last ingredient in this spice.

My boyfriend and I tried this on our pasta salad and we both agreed that it was barely detectable; meaning it was not hot nor spicy. It just added a light flavor. This will definitely go on eggs and, since it's so light, on fish. However, it is very mild.

The ingredients are black sesame seeds, white sesame seeds, poppy seeds, dehydrated garlic, dehydrated onion, red pepper, and salt.

NOTE - There is no plastic top on the jars. There needs to be a top with spice holes so that this can be shaken on food. Instead I have to be very careful I do not over "salt" my dishes with this because it will just spill out.

The current price of $17.99 for two comes to an expansive $9 per jar. In summary, it is a good flavor but very delicate compared to what I expected.

title: Delicious tea w/balanced flavor profile; lavender predominant; amber brew
text:
The fragrance of this tea in the bag is intoxicating to me. The ingredients are ceylon black tea, natural oil of bergamot, and lavender flowers. The ingredients are sourced from Sri Lanka and China.

Per the instructions, I brewed the tea at 185 degrees Fahrenheit and steeped it for 5 minutes. The instructions say 3-5 minutes. Note that I normally brew black tea with boiling water but due to the lavender flowers, I believe the temperature has to be reduced.

The resulting brew was amber and the taste was very balanced with lavender the predominant flavor. There are 30 corn fiber pyramid tea bags in this pouch for the current price of $13.99 which comes to $.47 cents per cup.

title: Deeply aromatic & intensely spicey; only 1.08 ounces
text:
This is a product of China distributed by Natural Plus Green from Flushing NY. The bottle is small at 1.08 ounces.

These ground cloves are deeply aromatic and intensely spicey. This will work well in my cranberry apple chutney or in apple pie. It will also be good on ham. A little goes a long way.

The current price of $6.99 is competitively priced for this as the McCormick ground cloves at my local store are $5.99 for 0.9 ounces and the Badia ground cloves are $2.49 for 1.75 ounces.

title: Tastes like a basic green tea served at a Chinese restaurant
text:
There are 30 silken tea bags in this package. This is their organic green tea which comes from South Africa.

I brewed the tea with 176 degree water and let it steep for three minutes. It brewed to a deep yellow hue. This tastes like the tea I get at Chinese restaurants. Just basic typical green tea. Note that I normally brew my green tea at 180 degrees but I can tell this would not be good with this tea as it would be too bitter.

The current price of $18.99 for 30 bags comes to $.63 cents per cup.

title: Brews to pale yellow after 9-minute steep; flavor & aroma subtly spicey
text:
This tin can contains 3.9 ounces of loose leaf turmeric and ginger tea. It also comes with a cute little wooden teaspoon. Note that this is actually a tisane and not a tea as there are no leaves from the Camellia sinensis evergreen plant which is what real tea is made of. Nevertheless, I really like this brew.

Per the instructions, I heated my water to 205 degrees and steeped it for 9 minutes. The resulting brew is a pale yellow with a subtle but spicey aroma and flavor that is pleasing to the palate.

The ingredients are turmeric root, ginger root, organic lemongrass, licorice root, orange peel, black peppercorn, and essential lemon oil.

Two negatives I see with this. First of all, it is very difficult to remove the top from the tin. I almost spilled the tea all over the place. They need to do something about that. Second, if one inhales the tea directly from the container, it may cause a bout of sneezing. I inhaled deeply as I really liked the aroma which caused me to sneeze repeatedly.

The current price of $14.99 is competitively priced for this. Note this is a fair trade tea and the company is based in Canada but the ingredients are grown elsewhere.

title: Not my favorite bergamot flavor but not bad either
text:
There are 30 silken tea bags in this package. I tried the earl grey which is one of my favorite teas. However, the fragrance of the earl grey smells more like St. Joseph's baby aspirin than the bergamot aroma I am used to in the US. This comes from South Africa.

I brewed the tea with boiling water and let it steep for five minutes. It brewed to the typical auburn brown hue of earl grey black tea. The taste was like drinking St. Joseph's baby aspirin that I remember from when I was a kid. Since I like that taste, I consider this an okay brew but it is not my favorite flavor of earl grey.

The current price of $18.99 for 30 bags comes to $.63 cents per cup.

title: Ingredients are lemon balm & Stevia; not my cup of tea
text:
There are 15 tea bags in this package. The ingredients are lemon balm and Stevia.

I do not use artificial sugars; in fact I do not usually use sugar at all. Had I known this had Stevia in it I would not have ordered it. However, I also do not like the smell or taste of the tea. The tea smells like dirty socks to me and tastes a little bit like Chamomile, which I absolutely hate.  It brews to a light yellow. This is definitely not my cup of tea.
